learn.title: Comparing Types of Solar Panels
learn.intro: Choosing the right solar technology is critical for maximizing energy harvest and reducing costs. Understanding the trade-offs between efficiency, cost, and space allows homeowners and developers to optimize their energy transition strategy, ensuring that the most effective hardware is deployed for specific environments to accelerate the shift away from fossil fuels.
learn.c1_text: Made from a single pure silicon crystal, these are the 'premium' option. Like a high-performance sports car, they offer the best efficiency, often exceeding 20%. They take up less space but cost more, making them ideal for residential rooftops where every square inch counts.
learn.c2_text: Polycrystalline panels are made from melted silicon fragments, acting like a reliable family sedan—cheaper but slightly less efficient (15-17%). Thin-film is flexible and lightweight, like a solar 'sticker,' making it perfect for large-scale industrial warehouses or curved surfaces where weight is a concern.
learn.c3_text: India's National Solar Mission has pushed massive adoption of polycrystalline panels due to lower costs, though a shift toward high-efficiency Mono-PERC cells is growing. The PLI scheme encourages domestic manufacturing to reduce reliance on imports and lower the cost per watt.

learn.title: The Hydrogen Colour Code Explained
learn.intro: Hydrogen is a versatile energy carrier that can decarbonize 'hard-to-abate' sectors like steel and shipping. The 'colour code' is a shorthand used by engineers and policymakers to identify the carbon footprint of the production process, which is essential for determining if the fuel is truly sustainable or just a bridge technology.
learn.c1_text: Produced via electrolysis powered by renewables, this is the gold standard. Think of it as 'distilling' water using wind or solar power. It emits zero CO2 during production, creating a completely closed-loop clean energy cycle for heavy industry.
learn.c2_text: Grey hydrogen comes from natural gas and releases CO2. Blue hydrogen uses the same process but adds Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) to trap emissions. It's like using a vacuum cleaner to catch the dust before it enters the room, reducing but not eliminating the footprint.
learn.c3_text: The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for production. With a target of 5 MMT per annum by 2030, India leverages its low-cost solar power to make green hydrogen economically competitive against fossil-fuel alternatives.
learn.stat2: Grey H2: Most common current method
learn.stat4: India Target: 5 MMT per annum by 2030

learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifespan and Recycling
learn.intro: As the first generation of wind farms reaches the end of its life, the industry faces a waste challenge. Managing the decommissioning process and recycling composite blades is vital to ensure that wind energy remains a truly circular economy rather than creating a landfill crisis of fiberglass.
learn.c1_text: Most turbines last 20 to 25 years. Maintenance is like a car service; regular lubrication and blade inspections prevent catastrophic failure. Over time, 'repowering' occurs, where old turbines are replaced with newer, more powerful models on the same site.
learn.c2_text: Blades are made of composite resins and fiberglass, which are notoriously hard to break down. New techniques involve chemical recycling or shredding them for use in cement kilns, turning old blades into building materials for roads and bridges.
learn.c3_text: India has one of the largest wind capacities globally, with many early installations in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat now needing repowering. Policy frameworks are evolving to manage the decommissioning of these early-stage turbines sustainably.
learn.stat3: Repowering: Increases energy yield per site

learn.title: Harvesting Energy from Ocean Waves
learn.intro: The ocean is the world's largest untapped energy reservoir. Wave energy converters capture the kinetic energy of moving water to generate electricity, providing a more predictable power source than wind or solar, which is crucial for stabilizing the grid in coastal regions.
learn.c1_text: These are floating buoys that move up and down with the waves. Like a piston in an engine, this linear motion drives a generator to create electricity. They are highly scalable and can be deployed in arrays across the open sea.
learn.c2_text: These use a chamber where waves push air through a turbine. As the water level rises and falls, it acts like a giant bellows, forcing air through a Wells turbine that spins regardless of the air flow direction.
learn.c3_text: With a coastline of over 7,500 km, India has immense potential for wave and tidal energy. Research is focusing on the Gujarat and Tamil Nadu coasts to supplement the grid for remote fishing communities and desalination plants.

learn.title: Floating Solar Farms (FPV)
learn.intro: Floating Photovoltaics (FPV) solve the problem of land scarcity by placing solar panels on reservoirs and lakes. This dual-use approach optimizes space and creates a symbiotic relationship between the water and the panels, improving efficiency and conserving precious water resources.
learn.c1_text: Water acts as a natural coolant for the panels. Because solar panels lose efficiency as they overheat, the water's cooling effect is like an air conditioner, increasing energy yield by up to 5-10% compared to land-based systems.
learn.c2_text: Panels shade the water, significantly reducing evaporation. This is like putting a lid on a pot; it keeps the water in the reservoir, which is critical for irrigation and drinking water during droughts.
learn.c3_text: India is deploying massive floating solar plants on hydroelectric dams. This 'hybrid' approach uses the same transmission lines for both hydro and solar, reducing infrastructure costs, as seen in projects in Telangana and Kerala.

learn.title: The Science of the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: The photoelectric effect is the fundamental physics principle that makes solar power possible. By converting light directly into electricity without any moving parts, it represents one of the most elegant and efficient ways to harvest energy from the sun.
learn.c1_text: Light consists of particles called photons. When a photon hits a silicon cell, it's like a billiard ball hitting another, knocking an electron loose from its atom. This creates a free electron ready to move.
learn.c2_text: Solar cells use two layers of silicon (P-type and N-type) to create an internal electric field. This field acts like a one-way street, forcing the loose electrons to flow in a specific direction, creating a DC electrical current.
learn.c3_text: India is scaling its PV manufacturing through the PLI scheme to master the entire value chain, from polysilicon to modules, reducing the cost of the photoelectric conversion process for millions of rural households.
learn.stat4: India: Massive PLI support for PV
